label: Acetyl-CoA
summary: Central metabolic precursor derived from glucose catabolism via pyruvate dehydrogenase. Entry point into the mevalonate pathway for isoprenoid biosynthesis in engineered S. cerevisiae.
toxicity_impact: None — low molecular weight primary metabolite, rapidly consumed in central metabolism
audit_trail: Ro et al. 2006, Figure 1 — MVA pathway entry point; no accumulation risk reported
cofactor_balance: Generates 1 NADH + 1 CO₂ via pyruvate dehydrogenase complex per acetyl-CoA formed
gene_recommendation: OE: ACS1/ACS2 — acetyl-CoA synthetase overexpression increases cytosolic acetyl-CoA pool for MVA pathway
dsp_bottleneck: None — central metabolite, not a DSP concern
ic50_toxicity: No growth inhibition — rapidly consumed primary metabolite

label: HMG-CoA
summary: 3-Hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA is the substrate of HMG-CoA reductase (HMGR), the rate-limiting enzyme of the mevalonate pathway. Overexpression of tHMGR bypasses feedback inhibition.
toxicity_impact: Minimal — CoA-thioester rapidly turned over by HMGR; accumulation may cause CoA sequestration stress in yeast
audit_trail: Ro et al. 2006, Supplementary — tHMGR overexpression prevents HMG-CoA accumulation
cofactor_balance: Consumes 1 ATP (acetoacetyl-CoA → HMG-CoA condensation step is ATP-independent but precursor formation requires acetyl-CoA)
gene_recommendation: OE: tHMGR (truncated HMG-CoA reductase) — bypasses feedback inhibition, rate-limiting step of MVA pathway
dsp_bottleneck: None — CoA-thioester intermediate, not found in culture supernatant
ic50_toxicity: No direct toxicity; CoA sequestration stress possible at >10 mM

label: Mevalonate
summary: First committed intermediate of the mevalonate pathway. Upregulation of HMGR dramatically boosted mevalonate production in the engineered S. cerevisiae strain.
toxicity_impact: None — water-soluble organic acid, rapidly phosphorylated by MVK; no toxicity at pathway-relevant concentrations
audit_trail: Ro et al. 2006, Figure 1 — committed intermediate; mevalonate kinase keeps pool low
cofactor_balance: Consumes 2 NADPH (HMG-CoA reductase catalyzes 2-electron reduction per mevalonate formed)
gene_recommendation: OE: ERG12 (mevalonate kinase) — prevents mevalonate accumulation; KO: BTS1 — blocks GGPP competing branch
dsp_bottleneck: None — water-soluble organic acid, easily separated from hydrophobic terpenoids
ic50_toxicity: No toxicity at pathway-relevant concentrations (<5 mM)

label: FPP
summary: Universal C15 isoprenoid building block and pivotal branch-point metabolite. Ro et al. re-directed FPP away from sterol biosynthesis by downregulating ERG9 via a MET3 promoter.
toxicity_impact: Moderate concern — FPP accumulation can disrupt membrane prenylation and sterol homeostasis; ERG9 downregulation creates ergosterol-deficient stress
audit_trail: Ro et al. 2006, Methods — ERG9 repression via MET3 promoter; FPP is the key branch-point
cofactor_balance: Consumes 3 ATP total (mevalonate → IPP requires 3 phosphorylation steps; IPP → FPP is cofactor-neutral)
gene_recommendation: KO: ERG9 (squalene synthase) — blocks FPP diversion to sterol pathway; OE: ADS — pulls FPP towards amorphadiene
dsp_bottleneck: Moderate — FPP pyrophosphate hydrolysis products may co-extract with sesquiterpenes
ic50_toxicity: FPP accumulation disrupts membrane prenylation at >2 mM; ERG9 KO requires ergosterol supplementation

label: epi-Cedrol
summary: Sesquiterpene alcohol produced by competing cyclisation of FPP via endogenous yeast terpene cyclases. Major impurity in amorphadiene-producing strains. Structurally dissimilar to artemisinin but co-extracted in organic solvents.
citation: Ro et al., 2006. Nature 440, 940–943; Jackson et al., 2003. Org. Lett. 5, 1629–1632.
toxicity_impact: Low direct toxicity but competes for FPP substrate pool, reducing artemisinin precursor yield by up to 15%; requires additional chromatographic purification step
audit_trail: Side reaction: FPP → epi-cedrol via endogenous yeast sesquiterpene cyclase; Ro et al. 2006, Supplementary Fig. 3
cofactor_balance: Cofactor-neutral — cyclisation of FPP is an intramolecular rearrangement requiring no external cofactors
gene_recommendation: KO: YJL167W (endogenous terpene cyclase) — eliminates competing FPP cyclisation to epi-cedrol
dsp_bottleneck: Similar logP to amorphadiene — co-extracted in organic solvent; requires chromatographic separation
ic50_toxicity: Low direct toxicity; no growth inhibition below 20 mM

label: Amorphadiene
summary: First committed sesquiterpene intermediate specific to the artemisinin branch. Produced by amorphadiene synthase (ADS) from FPP via 1,10 ring closure and 1,3 hydride shift.
toxicity_impact: None — volatile hydrocarbon, readily evaporates from culture; no cellular toxicity reported at production levels
audit_trail: Ro et al. 2006, Figure 2 — ADS cyclisation product; MW 204.35 vs artemisinin 282.33 (easily separated by boiling point)
cofactor_balance: Cofactor-neutral — ADS-catalyzed cyclisation of FPP releases pyrophosphate (PPi); no NAD(P)H or ATP consumed
gene_recommendation: OE: ADS (amorphadiene synthase) — high-copy expression pulls FPP flux towards artemisinin branch
dsp_bottleneck: Volatile C15 hydrocarbon — boiling point differs from artemisinin; gas-phase stripping feasible
ic50_toxicity: No cellular toxicity — volatile, readily evaporates from culture

label: Artemisinic Acid
summary: Direct precursor to artemisinin, produced by three-step oxidation of amorphadiene by CYP71AV1. Accumulates extracellularly in engineered yeast for easy solvent extraction.
toxicity_impact: Low host toxicity (exported extracellularly); however, high structural similarity to artemisinin (MW 234.33 vs 282.33, identical carbon skeleton) makes chromatographic separation challenging
audit_trail: Ro et al. 2006, Figure 3 — CYP71AV1 oxidation product; same C15 sesquiterpene skeleton as artemisinin; separation requires careful HPLC optimization
cofactor_balance: Consumes 3 NADPH + 3 O₂ (CYP71AV1 performs three sequential oxidations: amorphadiene → artemisinic alcohol → aldehyde → acid)
gene_recommendation: OE: CYP71AV1 + CPR1 (cytochrome P450 reductase) — increases oxidation throughput; balance with NADPH regeneration via ZWF1 OE
dsp_bottleneck: High — identical C15 sesquiterpene skeleton as artemisinin (MW 234 vs 282); co-elutes on reverse-phase HPLC
ic50_toxicity: Low host toxicity — exported extracellularly; no IC50 concern below 25 mM

label: Arteannuin B
summary: Sesquiterpene lactone byproduct formed by non-enzymatic rearrangement of dihydroartemisinic acid. Structural analog of artemisinin — the most problematic impurity for downstream purification due to near-identical physicochemical properties.
citation: Ro et al., 2006. Nature 440; Brown & Sy, 2007. Tetrahedron 63, 9548–9566.
toxicity_impact: Potential genotoxicity risk — sesquiterpene lactone with α-methylene-γ-butyrolactone moiety can alkylate biological nucleophiles; must be reduced below ICH Q3A threshold (0.15%) in API purification
audit_trail: Brown & Sy 2007 — arteannuin B co-crystallises with artemisinin during recrystallisation; MW 248.32 vs artemisinin 282.33; identical sesquiterpene skeleton; separation_cost_index computed from Tanimoto similarity 0.91
cofactor_balance: Cofactor-neutral — non-enzymatic rearrangement of dihydroartemisinic acid; spontaneous oxidative degradation product
gene_recommendation: N/A — non-enzymatic byproduct; mitigation via process control (temperature, pH) rather than genetic engineering
dsp_bottleneck: Critical — near-identical physicochemical properties to artemisinin; co-crystallises during recrystallisation; Tanimoto similarity 0.91
ic50_toxicity: < 10 μM growth inhibition — sesquiterpene lactone alkylates biological nucleophiles; potential genotoxicity

label: Artemisinin
summary: Sesquiterpene endoperoxide lactone — the most effective first-line antimalarial. Ro et al. 2006 produced a scalable microbial supply of its precursor artemisinic acid.
citation: Ro et al., 2006. Nature 440, 940–943. WHO Essential Medicine since 2002.
toxicity_impact: Target product — therapeutic sesquiterpene; no purification concern as this IS the desired API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient)
audit_trail: Ro et al. 2006 — final target product; MW 282.33; reference standard for all separation cost calculations
cofactor_balance: Net pathway balance from glucose: −3 ATP, −5 NADPH, +1 NADH per artemisinin molecule (total MVA + oxidation steps)
gene_recommendation: OE: ZWF1 (glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase) — increases NADPH supply for CYP71AV1 oxidation steps; KO: ERG9 — maximizes FPP pool
dsp_bottleneck: None — this IS the target API; all separation cost indices reference this compound
ic50_toxicity: Therapeutic compound — no purification toxicity concern; IC50 for Plasmodium ~10 nM (desired activity)
